questions.question:
G is a graph on n vertices and 2n - 2 edges. The edges of G can be partitioned into two edge-disjoint spanning trees. Which of the following is NOT true for G?

A. For every subset of k vertices, the induced subgraph has at most 2k - 2 edges.
B. The minimum cut in G has at least 2 edges.
C. There are at least 2 edge-disjoint paths between every pair of vertices.
D. There are at least 2 vertex-disjoint paths between every pair of vertices.
questions.answer: D. There are at least 2 vertex-disjoint paths between every pair of vertices.
questions.to_find: Which statement is NOT necessarily true when G is the union of two edge-disjoint spanning trees

understand.plain: When a graph can be split into two edge-disjoint spanning trees, it is said to have edge-connectivity at least 2. Edge-disjoint paths (at least 2) between every pair of vertices follow directly, but vertex-disjoint paths require vertex-connectivity >= 2, which is a stronger condition not implied by the two-tree decomposition alone. A graph can be 2-edge-connected but only 1-vertex-connected (have a cut vertex).

keywords.explain: Two spanning trees that share no edges; their union covers all n vertices using 2(n-1) = 2n-2 edges.
keywords.example: K_4 has 6 edges and can be split into 2 spanning trees each with 3 edges.

keywords.explain: Minimum number of edges whose removal disconnects the graph; having 2 spanning trees implies edge-connectivity >= 2.
keywords.example: Removing any single edge leaves the second spanning tree intact, so G stays connected.

keywords.explain: Paths between u and v that share no internal vertices; requires vertex-connectivity >= 2, not just edge-connectivity >= 2.
keywords.example: A star graph K_{1,3} has edge-connectivity 1 but the center is a cut vertex.

understand.visual_alt: A graph decomposed into two spanning trees (blue and red edges), illustrating 2-edge-connectivity while a cut vertex may still exist.

given.aim: Identify which property does NOT follow from G being decomposable into two edge-disjoint spanning trees.

terms.connects: Each spanning tree uses n-1 edges; two trees use 2n-2 edges total

terms.meaning: Total edges in G, exactly enough for two spanning trees
terms.example: For n=4: 6 edges = two spanning trees each with 3 edges

terms.meaning: Minimum edges whose removal disconnects G
terms.example: If each spanning tree has at least one edge crossing any cut, min-cut >= 2
terms.connects: Each spanning tree is connected, so any cut has at least one edge in each tree

given.plan: Verify options A, B, C are true from spanning-tree theory, then show D can fail by constructing a counterexample or citing Menger's theorem (edge vs vertex connectivity).

steps.title: Verify option A (induced subgraph bound)
steps.formula_raw: A forest with n vertices and k components has n - k edges
steps.apply: Any spanning tree restricted to k vertices forms a forest with at most k-1 edges. Two trees together give at most 2(k-1) = 2k-2 edges in the induced subgraph. So A is TRUE.
steps.note: This is a standard property of 2-arboricity (Nash-Williams theorem).

steps.title: Verify option B (minimum cut >= 2)
steps.formula_raw: min-cut >= number of edge-disjoint spanning trees
steps.apply: Any cut separates G into two parts. Each spanning tree must have at least one edge crossing the cut. Since the two trees are edge-disjoint, at least 2 edges cross any cut. So min-cut >= 2 and B is TRUE.
steps.note: By Nash-Williams theorem, the graph has 2 edge-disjoint spanning trees iff every cut has >= 2 edges.

steps.title: Verify option C (2 edge-disjoint paths)
steps.formula_raw: Menger's theorem: max edge-disjoint paths = min edge cut
steps.apply: Since min edge-cut >= 2, by Menger's theorem there exist at least 2 edge-disjoint paths between every pair of vertices. C is TRUE.
steps.note: Edge-disjoint paths follow from edge-connectivity.

steps.title: Check option D (vertex-disjoint paths)
steps.apply: Edge-connectivity >= 2 does NOT imply vertex-connectivity >= 2. A graph with a cut vertex (vertex-connectivity = 1) can still have edge-connectivity 2. Counterexample: two triangles sharing a single vertex — each cut has >=2 edges but the shared vertex is a cut vertex. D is NOT necessarily true.
steps.note: Vertex-disjoint paths require vertex-connectivity >= 2, a stronger condition than edge-connectivity >= 2.

solution.result: D. There are at least 2 vertex-disjoint paths between every pair of vertices.

questions.formula_note: The key insight is Menger's theorem and the distinction between edge-connectivity and vertex-connectivity; two edge-disjoint spanning trees guarantee edge-connectivity >= 2 but NOT vertex-connectivity >= 2.

questions.question:
The line graph L(G) of a simple graph G is defined as follows:
- There is exactly one vertex v(e) in L(G) for each edge e in G.
- For any two edges e and e' in G, L(G) has an edge between v(e) and v(e'), if and only if e and e' are incident with the same vertex in G.

Which of the following statements is/are TRUE?
(P) The line graph of a cycle is a cycle.
(Q) The line graph of a clique is a clique.
(R) The line graph of a planar graph is planar.
(S) The line graph of a tree is a tree.

A. P only
B. P and R only
C. R only
D. P, Q and S only
questions.to_find: Which statements about line graphs are true

understand.plain: The line graph L(G) represents adjacency of edges in G: each edge becomes a vertex, and two vertices in L(G) are adjacent when the original edges share an endpoint. This transforms edge-adjacency into vertex-adjacency. A cycle's edges form a cycle in L(G), but other properties like planarity and tree-ness do not carry over.

keywords.explain: L(G) has one vertex per edge of G; two vertices adjacent in L(G) iff the corresponding edges share an endpoint in G.
keywords.example: L(K_3) = K_3 since every pair of edges in a triangle shares a vertex.

keywords.explain: In a cycle C_n, each edge is adjacent to exactly 2 others; so L(C_n) is again a cycle C_n.
keywords.example: L(C_4) = C_4: each of the 4 edges touches exactly 2 neighbors cyclically.

keywords.explain: A tree on n vertices has n-1 edges; L(T) has n-1 vertices. L(T) may have cycles if a vertex has degree >= 3.
keywords.example: Star K_{1,3} has 3 edges all sharing center; L(K_{1,3}) = K_3, which has a triangle — not a tree.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='420' height='200' viewBox='0 0 420 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='420' height='200' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='70' y='25' class='lbl ink'>C_4</text><polygon points='60,60 120,40 160,80 100,100' fill='none' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='85' y='48' class='sub mut'>e1</text><text x='143' y='65' class='sub mut'>e2</text><text x='125' y='98' class='sub mut'>e3</text><text x='55' y='83' class='sub mut'>e4</text><text x='230' y='25' class='lbl ink'>L(C_4) = C_4</text><circle cx='270' cy='60' r='8' class='hi'/><circle cx='330' cy='60' r='8' class='hi'/><circle cx='330' cy='120' r='8' class='hi'/><circle cx='270' cy='120' r='8' class='hi'/><line x1='278' y1='60' x2='322' y2='60' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='330' y1='68' x2='330' y2='112' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='278' y1='120' x2='322' y2='120' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='270' y1='68' x2='270' y2='112' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='260' y='50' class='sub mut'>v(e1)</text><text x='332' y='50' class='sub mut'>v(e2)</text><text x='332' y='132' class='sub mut'>v(e3)</text><text x='242' y='132' class='sub mut'>v(e4)</text><text x='30' y='170' class='sub ink'>P is TRUE: L(C_n) = C_n for any cycle. Q,R,S can fail.</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Diagram showing C_4 on the left and its line graph L(C_4) = C_4 on the right, illustrating that the line graph of a cycle is a cycle.

given.aim: Determine which of the four statements P, Q, R, S about line graphs are true.

terms.meaning: Line graph of G; one vertex per edge of G, edges between adjacent edges of G
terms.example: L(path of 3 vertices) = path of 2 vertices (edge between the two edges of the path)
In C_n each edge has exactly 2 neighbors, so L(C_n) = C_n
L(K_4) has C(4,2)=6 vertices; edges not forming K_6 because non-adjacent edges in K_4 exist; e.g. edge {1,2} and edge {3,4} share no vertex

given.plan: Check each statement: P by cycle structure, Q by checking non-incident edges in cliques, R by a known counterexample (K_5 is planar base but L(K_5) is non-planar), S by a degree-3 vertex in a tree.

steps.title: Check P: L(cycle) is a cycle
steps.formula_raw: In L(G), two vertices adjacent iff original edges share an endpoint
steps.apply: In C_n, every edge is incident with exactly 2 other edges (one on each side). So each vertex of L(C_n) has degree 2, and L(C_n) is connected — hence L(C_n) = C_n. P is TRUE.
steps.note: This works for all n >= 3.

steps.title: Check Q: L(clique) is a clique
steps.formula_raw: In K_n, two edges are non-incident iff they form a matching
steps.apply: In K_4, edges {1,2} and {3,4} share no vertex, so they are NOT adjacent in L(K_4). Hence L(K_4) is not K_6. Q is FALSE (for n >= 4).
steps.note: L(K_3) = K_3 but L(K_n) for n >= 4 is not a complete graph.

steps.title: Check R: L(planar graph) is planar
steps.apply: K_4 is planar, but L(K_4) = K_{1,1,1,1,1,1} (octahedron graph), which is planar. However K_5 is planar and L(K_5) contains K_5 as minor and is non-planar. So R is FALSE.
steps.note: Not every planar graph has a planar line graph; Beineke's theorem characterises planar line graphs.

steps.title: Check S: L(tree) is a tree
steps.formula_raw: A forest with n vertices and k components has n-k edges
steps.apply: Star K_{1,3} is a tree with 3 edges. L(K_{1,3}) has 3 vertices (one per edge) all pairwise adjacent (all share the center vertex) = K_3, which is a cycle — not a tree. S is FALSE.
steps.note: L(T) is a tree only when T is a path.

questions.formula_note: The line-graph adjacency rule (two vertices of L(G) adjacent iff original edges share an endpoint) is the key formula; it immediately shows P is true and Q, S are false.

questions.question: Consider an undirected graph G where self-loops are not allowed. The vertex set of G is {(i, j) : 1 <= i <= 12, 1 <= j <= 12}. There is an edge between (a, b) and (c, d) if |a - c| <= 1 and |b - d| <= 1. The number of edges in this graph is ______.
questions.to_find: Total number of edges in the graph on a 12x12 grid with adjacency within distance 1 in each coordinate

understand.plain: The vertex set is a 12x12 grid. Two vertices (a,b) and (c,d) are adjacent when they differ by at most 1 in each coordinate — i.e., they are horizontal, vertical, or diagonal neighbours in the grid (like a king's moves in chess). We count all such pairs (excluding self-loops).

keywords.explain: Each interior cell connects to up to 8 neighbours (N, NE, E, SE, S, SW, W, NW), edge cells fewer, corners only 3.
keywords.example: Cell (6,6) connects to 8 neighbours; cell (1,1) connects to 3.

keywords.explain: Count horizontal edges, vertical edges, and diagonal edges (two directions) separately, then sum.
Each 2x2 sub-square contributes 2 diagonals; there are 11x11 = 121 such sub-squares.

x='220' y='100' class='sub no'>Vertical: red</text><text x='220' y='120' class='sub' style='fill:#7F77DD'>Diagonal: purple</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A 3x3 grid showing king-move edges: horizontal (green), vertical (red), and diagonal (purple) connections between adjacent cells.

given.aim: Count all edges in the 12x12 grid graph where vertices within king-move distance are adjacent.

terms.meaning: {(i,j) : 1 <= i <= 12, 1 <= j <= 12} — a 12x12 grid of 144 vertices
terms.connects: Each vertex has at most 8 neighbours

terms.meaning: Between (a,b) and (a, b+1); same row, adjacent columns
Between (a,b) and (a+1, b+1) or (a+1, b-1); across 2x2 sub-squares

given.plan: Count horizontal, vertical, and diagonal (both directions) edges separately using the grid dimensions, then sum.

steps.title: Count horizontal edges
steps.apply: Each of the 12 rows has 11 adjacent pairs (columns 1-2, 2-3, ..., 11-12). Horizontal edges = 12 * 11 = 132.
steps.note: These connect (a,b)-(a,b+1) for all valid a,b.

steps.title: Count vertical edges
steps.apply: Each of the 12 columns has 11 adjacent pairs. Vertical edges = 12 * 11 = 132.
steps.note: These connect (a,b)-(a+1,b) for all valid a,b.

steps.title: Count diagonal edges (both directions)
steps.apply: Each 2x2 sub-square (there are 11*11 = 121 of them) contributes 2 diagonal edges: one going down-right and one going down-left. Diagonal edges = 2 * 11 * 11 = 242.
steps.note: Direction 1: (a,b)-(a+1,b+1); Direction 2: (a,b)-(a+1,b-1).

steps.title: Sum all edges
steps.note: Verified: 506 edges in the 12x12 king-move graph.

questions.formula_note: No standard formula applies directly; the key is to separately count horizontal, vertical, and diagonal edges using grid dimensions: 12*11 + 12*11 + 2*11*11 = 506.

questions.question: The maximum number of edges in a bipartite graph on 12 vertices is ______.
questions.to_find: Maximum number of edges in a bipartite graph on 12 vertices

understand.plain: A bipartite graph splits its vertices into two independent sets, with edges only between the sets. To maximise edges, we use a complete bipartite graph K_{m,n} where m + n = 12. The product m * n is maximised when m = n = 6, giving K_{6,6} with 36 edges.

keywords.explain: Vertices split into sets A and B; all edges go between A and B, none within A or B.
Every vertex in A is adjacent to every vertex in B; has m*n edges.
For fixed m + n = v, the product m * n is maximised when m = n = v/2.
keywords.example: m + n = 12, max of m*n at m=n=6 gives 36.

understand.visual_alt: Complete bipartite graph K_{6,6} with two sets of 6 vertices each fully connected to the other side, giving 36 edges.

given.aim: Find the maximum number of edges in any bipartite graph on 12 vertices.

terms.meaning: Sizes of the two partition sets in the bipartite graph
Complete bipartite graph achieving maximum edges for given m, n

given.plan: Use the formula: max edges in bipartite graph on v vertices = floor(v^2 / 4), achieved by K_{v/2, v/2}.

steps.title: Apply max-edges bipartite formula
steps.apply: v = 12. Max edges = floor(12^2 / 4) = floor(144 / 4) = 36.
steps.note: This is achieved by K_{6,6} where m = n = 6.

steps.title: Verify with K_{6,6}
steps.apply: K_{6,6} has 6 * 6 = 36 edges. Any other split like K_{5,7} gives 5*7 = 35 < 36. So the maximum is 36.
steps.note: The balanced split always maximises the product for fixed sum.

questions.formula_note: The formula floor(v^2/4) gives the maximum edges in a bipartite graph on v vertices, achieved by the balanced complete bipartite graph K_{v/2, v/2}.

questions.question:
If G is a forest with n vertices and k connected components, how many edges does G have?

A. |n/k|
B. (n - k)/2
C. n - k
D. n + k - 1
questions.to_find: Number of edges in a forest with n vertices and k components

understand.plain: A forest is an acyclic graph (a collection of trees). Each connected component is a tree. A tree on m vertices has exactly m - 1 edges. Summing over all k components: if component i has n_i vertices, it contributes n_i - 1 edges. Total edges = sum of (n_i - 1) = (sum of n_i) - k = n - k.

keywords.explain: A graph with no cycles; each connected component is a tree.
keywords.example: Three isolated edges (paths of length 1) form a forest with 6 vertices, 3 components, and 3 = 6 - 3 edges.

keywords.explain: A tree on m vertices has exactly m - 1 edges.
keywords.example: A path of 5 vertices is a tree with 4 edges.

keywords.explain: A forest with n vertices and k components has n - k edges.

understand.visual_alt: Three separate tree components (k=3) each contributing m_i - 1 edges summing to n - k total edges.

given.aim: Express the number of edges in a forest in terms of n (vertices) and k (components).

terms.meaning: Total number of vertices in the forest
terms.connects: Sum of vertices across all k trees

terms.meaning: Number of connected components (trees) in the forest
terms.connects: Each component is a tree with n_i - 1 edges

terms.meaning: Number of vertices in the i-th tree component

given.plan: Sum the edge counts of each tree component: total edges = sum_{i=1}^{k} (n_i - 1) = n - k.

steps.title: Edge count per tree component
steps.formula_raw: A tree on m vertices has exactly m - 1 edges
steps.apply: Component i has n_i vertices and n_i - 1 edges. This holds for all k components.
steps.note: A single vertex is a tree with 0 edges.

steps.title: Sum over all components
steps.formula_raw: forest with n vertices and k components has n - k edges
steps.apply: Total edges = sum_{i=1}^{k} (n_i - 1) = (n_1 + n_2 + ... + n_k) - k = n - k.
steps.note: This confirms the answer n - k.

questions.formula_note: The forest edge formula (edges = n - k) is the direct generalisation of the tree formula (edges = n - 1) to multiple components.

questions.question:
In a connected graph, a bridge is an edge whose removal disconnects the graph. Which one of the following statements is true?

A. A tree has no bridges.
B. A bridge cannot be part of a simple cycle.
C. Every bridge in a graph is incident on a cut vertex.
D. A graph with bridges cannot have a cycle.
questions.answer: B. A bridge cannot be part of a simple cycle.
questions.to_find: The true statement about bridges in connected graphs

understand.plain: A bridge is a critical edge — removing it splits the graph. The key characterisation is that an edge is a bridge if and only if it does not lie on any cycle. So bridges cannot be part of any simple cycle. Trees are full of bridges (every edge is one), option C is sometimes true but not always, and option D is wrong since a graph can have both cycles and bridges.

keywords.explain: An edge whose removal increases the number of connected components; also called a cut edge.
keywords.example: In a path graph, every edge is a bridge.

keywords.explain: An edge is a bridge if and only if it lies on no simple cycle.
keywords.example: Edges inside a triangle cannot be bridges; an edge connecting two components is always a bridge.

keywords.explain: A vertex whose removal disconnects the graph; bridges are often near cut vertices but not always incident on them.
keywords.example: In a path A-B-C, B is a cut vertex; edges AB and BC are bridges. A bridge can be incident on a leaf (degree-1 vertex), not a cut vertex.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='380' height='180' viewBox='0 0 380 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='380' height='180' rx='10' class='bg'/><text x='10' y='22' class='lbl ink'>Bridge (dashed) vs cycle edge</text><circle cx='60' cy='90' r='10' class='hi'/><circle cx='130' cy='60' r='10' class='hi'/><circle cx='130' cy='120' r='10' class='hi'/><circle cx='200' cy='90' r='10' class='hi'/><circle cx='280' cy='60' r='10' class='hi'/><circle cx='280' cy='120' r='10' class='hi'/><line x1='70' y1='87' x2='120' y2='67' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='70' y1='93' x2='120' y2='113' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='130' y1='70' x2='130' y2='110' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='140' y1='68' x2='190' y2='88' stroke='#D85A30' stroke-width='3' stroke-dasharray='6,3'/><line x1='140' y1='112' x2='190' y2='92' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='210' y1='85' x2='270' y2='65' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='210' y1='95' x2='270' y2='115' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='280' y1='70' x2='280' y2='110' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><text x='185' y='78' class='sub no'>bridge</text><text x='10' y='160' class='sub ink'>Bridge (red dashed) lies on no cycle; cycle edges (green) are non-bridges.</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Graph showing a bridge (dashed red edge) connecting two subgraphs that each contain cycles; the bridge lies on no cycle while cycle edges cannot be bridges.

given.aim: Identify the true statement about bridges (cut edges) among the four options.

terms.meaning: Edge e such that G - e is disconnected
terms.example: Every edge in a path is a bridge
terms.connects: Equivalent to: e lies on no cycle

terms.term: Option A: trees have no bridges
terms.meaning: FALSE — every edge in a tree is a bridge
terms.example: In a path 1-2-3, edges (1,2) and (2,3) are both bridges

terms.term: Option C: every bridge incident on a cut vertex
terms.meaning: FALSE — a bridge to a leaf vertex: the leaf is not a cut vertex
terms.example: Path 1-2-3: edge (1,2) is a bridge; vertex 1 has degree 1 and is not a cut vertex

given.plan: Apply the bridge-cycle criterion: a bridge lies on no cycle. Evaluate each option against this and tree/graph theory facts.

steps.title: Evaluate option A
steps.formula_raw: e is a bridge iff e lies on no cycle
steps.apply: A tree is acyclic, so EVERY edge lies on no cycle — every edge of a tree is a bridge. Option A ('A tree has no bridges') is FALSE.
steps.note: A tree on n > 1 vertices has n-1 bridges.

steps.title: Evaluate option B
steps.formula_raw: e is a bridge iff e lies on no cycle
steps.apply: By the bridge-cycle criterion, if edge e lies on a simple cycle, then e is NOT a bridge (removing it leaves alternate paths). Conversely, a bridge cannot lie on any cycle. Option B is TRUE.
steps.note: This is the exact characterisation of bridges.

steps.title: Evaluate option C
steps.formula_raw: Cut vertex: vertex whose removal disconnects the graph
steps.apply: A bridge connecting to a leaf vertex: the leaf has degree 1 and is not a cut vertex, yet the edge is a bridge. Example: path 1-2-3, edge (1,2) is a bridge but vertex 1 is a leaf (not a cut vertex). Option C is FALSE.
steps.note: A bridge incident to a leaf is not incident on a cut vertex.

steps.title: Evaluate option D
steps.apply: A graph can have both cycles and bridges. Example: two triangles connected by a single edge — the connecting edge is a bridge, yet both triangles are cycles. Option D is FALSE.
steps.note: Bridges and cycles coexist in many graphs.

solution.result: B. A bridge cannot be part of a simple cycle.

questions.formula_note: The bridge-cycle criterion (an edge is a bridge iff it lies on no cycle) is the central theorem and directly proves option B is the only true statement.
